mappings.rationaleAI: Aesthetics examining art, beauty, taste, and aesthetic experience directly addresses the aesthetic experience (A), influences affecting apprehension of works of art (A.1), and the appreciation of works of art (A.3).

Art theory examining the interpretation of arts from classical aesthetics to contemporary practice addresses interpretation of works of art (A.2). Institutional definitions and postmodern discourse address the criticism of works of art (B) and diverse criteria of evaluation (B.1).

Philosophical investigation of beauty as a universal value addresses the aesthetic experience (A) and appreciation of works of art (A.3). Arguments about the rationality of aesthetic judgment address diverse criteria of evaluation (B.1).
Theory and practice of interpretation spanning textual and cultural communication directly addresses the interpretation of works of art (A.2) and meaning in art (A.2.a).

Literary theory analyzing text and meaning through structuralism and reader-response theory addresses interpretation of works of art (A.2). Systematic theoretical frameworks address the practice of criticism (B) and critical approaches to the arts (B.2.d).

Art history as a discipline studying visual arts development addresses scholarship in the arts (C), resources and methods of scholarship (C.1), and the relation of scholarship to other humanistic disciplines including history and archaeology (C.2).
Myth studied across anthropology, psychology, and literary criticism using structuralist and psychoanalytic approaches directly addresses symbol and myth in the arts (A.2.b).
Comparative world mythology examining universal motifs including deities, creation, and the hero directly addresses symbol and myth in the arts (A.2.b).

Critical theory covering the culture industry and alienation addresses the criticism of works of art (B), critical approaches to the arts (B.2.d), and the interaction of arts with social institutions (D).

Barthes's semiology and textual theory transforming cultural study addresses interpretation of works of art (A.2). The death of the author and pleasure of the text address critical approaches to the arts (B.2.d).
Poststructuralism challenging fixed meanings and stable identities addresses interpretation of works of art (A.2) and critical approaches to the arts (B.2.d).

Adorno's cultural criticism and analysis of capitalism's effects on music and arts addresses critical approaches to the arts (B.2.d) and the interaction of arts with social and economic institutions (D).
Contemporary art covering art market dynamics, museum branding, and commodification addresses the economics of art (E), factors affecting economic value (E.1), and the art market (E.3).

IP law covering copyright and creators' rights addresses remuneration of artists and protection of their rights (E.4). Digital technology challenges relate to fraudulence including piracy and plagiarism (E.5).
Free speech covering censorship, hate speech regulation, and digital censorship directly addresses social control of art including censorship and related regulation (D.2).
Journalism covering digital media, broadcasting, and online journalism addresses the role of mechanical and electronic media in disseminating works of art (G.4).

Reading covering censorship and the association of reading with subversive ideas addresses social control of art (D.2). The evolution of mass literacy through printing addresses preservation and dissemination of works of art (G).
Imagination as a cognitive power crucial to artistic and scientific creativity addresses the aesthetic experience (A) and interpretation of works of art (A.2).
Psychological perspectives on creativity and creative expression address the training and work of the artist (F), including the relationship between individual genius and cultural environment.
Education systems examining pedagogical methods and philosophical debates about education's purposes connect to the arts in education and aesthetic education (D.5).
Technology's role in music covering sound recording, broadcasting, and streaming addresses the role of mechanical and electronic media in dissemination (G.4).
Cinema history covering technological transitions and new distribution technologies addresses the role of mechanical and electronic media in dissemination (G.4).
Oral history covering interviewing techniques and archival preservation addresses the role of oral tradition in preserving works of art (G.5).
Comparative literature examining cross-cultural literary relations addresses scholarship in the arts (C) and the relation of scholarship to other humanistic disciplines (C.2).
Design covering the relationship between form and function, cultural context, and mass production addresses technology, science, and the arts (D.4).
Futurism's embrace of modern technology and its pioneering role in new media development addresses technology, science, and the arts (D.4).
